speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, survivor Morris Kesselman, K-E-S-S-E-L-M-A-N, May 1, 1996. You were telling us about your work at the camp. Can you tell me more about the daily routine at the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came into the Sonderkommando, the transports from Hungary were coming in one after the other. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw them coming in. And sometimes, most of the times, they took the men away from the women and children. They put them downstairs with several steps going down. The SS were standing around and directed them to a big chamber. The chamber is right in front of my eyes, huge. It had numbers with hooks, and also a lot of benches. The Germans were telling them not to forget their numbers, because when they come out, they can find their place. They undressed. And next to a hallway, there was the gas chamber. The gas chamber holded 2,500 people, because this was one of the largest one. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were all marched in there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the steel doors were closed. And the Zyklon was put up on the roof to a chimneys. There were several chimneys going down. And inside was with heavy mesh wire. And the Zyklon was coming through those wires. It couldn't take more than 15, 20 minutes. They were all people were dead. The gas chamber had a ventilation system with every five, six feet, an opening with big fans for drawing out the smell of the gas of whatever it was in there. Then the doors were opened up. When the doors were opened up, it's like a pile fell out through our opening, because they all piled towards the door, climbing on each other. Very filthy, dirty people, what they had to do with that dead body. And then, a certain amount of people from our commando, they were taking them out one by one and putting them next to an elevator going up one floor. And on top of the elevator, there was another commando taking them off the elevator and laying them out on the floor on the side of the ovens, four, five, the whole length in both sides where the ovens were. And one man on either side went out with a pair of pliers. They used to call them the dentists. And they were pulling out the gold teeth. This was going on day and night on a 12-hour base shifts. The people who worked in those crematoriums was 12 hours. And then the ovens had to be cooled-- didn't even have a chance to cool off, the ashes were taken out, outside with wheelbarrows, steel wheelbarrows. And there was another commando having in their hands stampers, it's a metal plate with a wooden stake-- and stamping the ashes and sifting them through a screen. Then another commando was loading them on trucks and wagons. And they pulled them towards the river. They dumped this in the river.

speaker: interviewer
text: What river was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The river was [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Vistula, Tistula, something Polish-- Vistula River something. In fact, a friend of mine who went back many years later brought some ashes. And we have a memorial in Brandeis University. And we have little ashes on our own buried in that particular place which came from Vistula River. I don't know exactly how you spell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Sonderkommando, were they were with these people when they first went into the undressing chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. If they were, maybe just a few got special-- some signias or bands that they can go in. Because, otherwise, you wouldn't know who was who. We wore civilian clothes. I did not wear the clothes with the lines. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But some of them were involved, not to the gas chambers, but where they undressed. Many times, it got over-warm in May. When the transports came, they didn't give them a chance. They undressed them upstairs in the yard. Then they didn't observe anymore separate men and women. They were so in a hurry that they undressed them on the top, because the clothes wasn't taken out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the people who came in realize what was happening and try to get away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody got away. But I don't think so. I think very few realized what's going to happen to them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not that I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was happening in the war at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rumors-- we had the Russians are progressing. We heard this, and we heard that, but we didn't know what's going to happen. We just had rumors until they evacuated us in January, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were doing this work with the Sonderkommandos, were you actually just staying near the crematoria? Were you able to go back into the rest of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I, for one, went in almost every evening. Because food had to be brought in for us. We did not have a kitchen in the Sonderkommando. But food wasn't a lacking thing, because people brought so much stuff for themselves. Especially from Hungary, they brought a lot of food. And there was no refrigerators. They did not stop us from taking the food. So we had plenty of food at that point. Only when the transports stop, we didn't have any food. But we had to go into the block to bring out some food and bring-- also buy whiskey for the Germans. You wouldn't believe it, but we actually bought, for the officers, whiskey in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you buy it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of the prisoners worked with civilian people. They did buy from them. And we bought it from them. Because money was no object. There was plenty of gold. And they actually gave us the stuff to buy for them. There were always drunk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, for one, went in most of the time. We took the whiskey. We put them in the kettles on a piece of string. Because we couldn't carry nothing with us, because we had to pass out the SS food. They never did search us. But if they did search us, we could have been killed. So this is the only reason we went into the camps. But otherwise, we slept in the crematoriums. We worked crematoriums until towards the end. You mentioned that you were able to cross a street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a laboratory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a laboratory was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, in particular, they brought in woman from Holland-- very chunky, heavy woman. And I got close to an SS man from Holland. And one time-- I don't know what made me ask him. And I says, does your family know what you're doing here. I used to bring him in the vodka, and I got to him very close. He says, no. My family doesn't know. So he told me, actually, that he saw some people from Holland brought in, women. And they did not gas them. They give them a needle in the neck on a table. And they took off some of the flesh from those people and put it in stainless steel tanks for the grafting, I assume, for soldiers. And he told me that. I actually did not see when these things was going on. But we went in to clean up, we saw the things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: The blood, this-- we saw the women being taken in. And some boys took out whatever was left to the crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of them alive when they were taken out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. They were, as I say, I was told they were given needles, a needle in the neck, or this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who was in charge of this kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Mengele was always involved there. I don't know if he is the one who was doing all of the work. But he-- I saw him practically every day coming in. He was wearing a white robe, always uniform, with a stick in his hand, knocking in the boots, and always smiling. But I assume he was in charge of this whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what his job was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Particular? No, I didn't know. I know he was in charge. We knew that he was the one going through the selections. People told us. Because we had friends, which in a commando, what they call Canada. They're the ones who took off the prisoners from the wagons. And they told us what he is doing over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That he is the one most around when the transport came in with a stick-- left, right, right, left. And he was in charge of this whole thing-- not the only one, but he was in charge of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said there was food as long as the transports were coming in. When did they stop coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Hungarian transport, the Warsaw ghetto came in. The Lodz ghetto start coming in August, 1944. But it wasn't in the hundreds of thousands. I mean, Lodz ghetto at that time wasn't several weeks. And after Lodz ghetto, there were Jewish people from Czechoslovakia-- whole families. They cleaned up death camp. This camp was called Camp C. And transports stopped. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you recognize any people from the Lodz ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I never was close to the people who came in. And I did not recognize anybody, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said they came in from Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones from Czechoslovakia were living in the camp quite a while, with the children, with their families, the whole business. Then they cleaned them all out. There also was a camp of Gypsies, which was cleaned out, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by cleaned out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They made-- they took everybody from the camp. They took the men out first. They sent them off someplace. Then they took the women with the children. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I want to bring up one point what happened to the people were in the camps when they couldn't carry on. There were selections practically every day, but not massive selections. As you went out through the gate, they could be pulled out to the side, out to the left. But once in a while, especially on holidays, they made selections. And I was in some of the selections, too. I was scared stiff. I was small. And I liked to put myself near small people. Because if I would stand near big people, I could have been the one taken. This was the worst part-- when they start bringing in prisoners, when you knew some of them, and they gas those people, this was bad. And also, they used to have womens' selections. And they brought them in bare naked on the trucks. And they opened up the trucks. And they dumped them like a coal wagon. Screaming was going on out of this world, because they knew where they're going. That was a horrible thing. This was the-- was worse than anything else. And then, also, on the end, they used to liquidate little camps around Auschwitz. And they brought in people half-dead. And they wouldn't even take them down to the gas chambers. Because there were no numerical-- were just maybe 200, 100, 150. And they brought them in. And two prisoners which worked in a crematorium camp, one holded each side of that prisoner. And one of the SS officer had a short shotgun behind his back. And they turned him around, and they gave him a bullet in the neck. And they threw him away like a piece of meat. They were never taken down to the gas chambers, right on the floor of the-- where the ovens were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think they did it that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have any respect for anybody. The reason why they did it, because there was-- the numbers weren't there. And they wouldn't spoil, dirty up the gas chamber. They wouldn't use so much Zyklon, the poison. I don't know why. They felt, what's the big deal, a bullet kills them, and that's it. And there was a couple of SS men. And they keep switching off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the SS men seem as though they did not really want to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, they did it very voluntarily. And another thing I want to bring up that with the ones I was, most of them were not Germans. The most of them came from [NON-ENGLISH], Latvia, Ukraine. They had German uniforms on. Very few German officers were among them all. Maybe one or two, the rest of them-- 80% was non-Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a time did the Sonderkommandos stay at their job? What was the usual length of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I came in, very few months they kept changing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean they kept changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed off and get some others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they always kill off?

speaker: interviewee
text: They always killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did they return them to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that when you went in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a fact. Oh, yes. I know that I'm not-- this is the end of the world. If ever the war comes before this, and from there, I can't get out anymore. But nobody gives up hope. You never know what's going to happen. But when we came in, there were 200 of the Sonderkommando. And from that day on, they did not change until the transports stopped. When the transports stopped, they tried to take out 400 of the 800. Those 400 they tried to take out was for crematorium three and four, which I was not there. I was in one and two. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the people knew where they're going. They know they're going to be killed. So they started an uprising. They burned down crematorium three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who started the uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: The prisoners from the Sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they able to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was rumors going around they were getting ready for an uprising. But it wasn't successful. But to burn down a wooden barracks, I mean, it was a lot of wood in there. So they put it to fire, and they start burning it. But the SS were ready with machine guns. And they killed 400 people out at that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they actually burn down one of the crematoria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That crematorium three was burned down. And until today, this is the only remains showing in Birkenau of the crematoriums, of the four crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you heard about the uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: We felt very good about it, because we thought its happened-- they were going to go. We didn't know who got killed and what got killed on the other side. They were a half a mile away from us. But it didn't take five minutes-- we were surrounded by the Germans, by the machine guns. So nothing actually happened in our crematoriums. Only thing what happened is they had one German, a kapo. So they killed him and slide him into the oven.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your ovens?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our crematorium, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the leaders-- it wasn't me, I was too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who the kapo was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our kapo, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially, there was Kaminski. Now, he was the lead kapo of all four crematoriums. He got killed-- we assumed he got killed-- about 10 days before the uprising. They came in and took him. The Germans, SS, came in and took him. They told him that we need him in the other crematorium. And he wore a uniform, a [NON-ENGLISH], a one-piece suit. And it had a little stick on his hand, like a cane. He was a short man. He was a very nice man. I think he was born in Poland, in [NON-ENGLISH]. He never came back. We knew what happened to him. Later on, rumors going around that the Germans knew that something might happen. And they accused him of being the leader of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the kapo that went into the oven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who did it? There was one special and one guy, Lemke. He lives now in Israel, in a kibbutz. He was my kapo. And I found out from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He tell us that that particular German kapo came from Lublin with 10 Russians. And he was sort of pain in the neck to them. And he gave them hard time. And they said that the first chance they will have, they get rid of him. And this was the chance to get rid of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the one they got rid of?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kapo? No, he was German. And I wouldn't even know his first name. He was not long with us. Because when they brought him from Lublin with the 10 Russians, was-- I don't know exactly, but it couldn't be more than a month before the end of the crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he then the only German that went into the oven?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the only German kapo. There were 10 Russians, like I said. But after there was the uprising, after the revolution, they took the 10 Russians, and they hanged them. They said that they were the leaders, and they were the instigators, because a Jew would never make an uprising. They didn't want to go out to the world history, if ever happens, that the Jews had an uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that was true, that the Russians--

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely not. They didn't-- we wouldn't even trust them. And they were such a short time. And the managers said, we wouldn't trust them what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that shortly after the uprising, your crematoria were surrounded by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was surrounded by Germans. And in a few hours, was all quiet down. And a few days later, they told us that we are going to go back to camp, live in a camp. There's no transports coming in. But they left 50 of us working in crematorium four, which was a small crematorium, just to burn the dead bodies. And we went back. But when we went back, we didn't take any blankets with us or nothing. And once we came into the camp, there was nothing for us. So the block-- all the elders said, you know, we got to go back to the crematorium and bring all the blankets. And I had hidden away certain moneys and other things. And the kapo which was very close to me, Lemke, said to me, you know, Morris, you come with me. And we took several wagons, which we-- you know, we-- not without horses, to bring the blankets. And I took a certain amount of money there in case we can get some food for it. Sometimes you have to pay $100 for one egg if you had an opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the money was brought in by the people. They stashed it in those holes. We found in the clothes. They threw it away-- diamonds, they brought a lot of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back then with your wagons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I went with the wagon, we had several wagons. And we had to pass the bath, the sauna bath, where the people gets bathed. On the way back, 200 of our boys were standing in the line already.

speaker: interviewer
text: In line where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there, to be bathed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean to go into the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no gas chambers. From the 400 what we all were left, 50 of us in the camp, in the crematorium, 150 went in the camp, and the 200 they took out from the 400 were already lined up to they take them away someplace. And luckily, they took the older numbers. There was a lot of Greek Jews with us. They had the new numbers. If I wouldn't have been gone with that wagon bringing it across, I would have been in it. And they actually killed them. There's no question about it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So sheer luck that I'm alive today. Where did you go with your blankets, then, when you went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to the barrack. And this was a barrack where, again, we were only put separate with a separate barrack. And we were watched by SS men. But we went every morning to the crematoriums to dismantle that crematoriums. I don't know why. They could have blown them up with one minute. But they made us all take off the steel gates and put them on crates. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we worked there quite a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know why the Germans were doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think now why they did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were crazy. Because the Russians were so close. We knew the Russians are close. That-- where are they going to go? They know the war is going very badly for them. But I assume they had orders from the higher up that made us do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said there were no more gas chambers by then.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gas-- as I say, we dismantled the doors. Only one gas chamber was kept alive, only for the dead people. No transports were coming in, no Jews to be brought in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said one crematoria was kept alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I asked you about the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The gas chambers were just abandoned. I mean, we didn't go down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just left it alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't try to destroy that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not while I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll pause now--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: To change the tape.
